title: Logging
subtitle: The logging module is your SLF4J

sections.java: // SLF4J: one logger per class import org.slf4j.Logger; import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory; private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(App.class); log.info("Loaded {} rows", count);
sections.python: # One logger per module, named __name__ import logging log = logging.getLogger(__name__) log.info("Loaded %s rows", count) # %s args are formatted lazily — only if # this level is actually enabled

sections.title: Don't print() for Diagnostics — and Configure Once
sections.content: print() is the Java equivalent of System.out.println debugging: no levels, no timestamps, no routing. Use logging. The key rule: libraries only call getLogger(__name__); the application configures handlers once at startup via logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO). Configuring logging inside library code (or calling basicConfig twice) is the classic mistake — mirror how you'd never call BasicConfigurator from a library in Java.

sections.title: Use %s Args, Not f-strings, in Log Calls
sections.content: Write log.debug("user=%s", user), not log.debug(f"user={user}"). The %s form is formatted lazily — the string is only built if that level is enabled, just like SLF4J's {} placeholders. An f-string is evaluated eagerly every call, paying the cost even when DEBUG is off.

sections.java: // Config in logback.xml / log4j2.xml // levels: TRACE DEBUG INFO WARN ERROR log.debug("detail"); log.warn("degraded"); log.error("failed", exception);
sections.python: # Configure once at the entry point logging.basicConfig( level=logging.INFO, format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s", ) log.debug("detail") log.warning("degraded") log.exception("failed") # inside except: adds traceback

sections.question: Where should logging be configured (handlers, level, format) in a Python app?
options: Once, at the application entry point — libraries should only call getLogger()
Inside each function before it logs
It is configured automatically; you never call basicConfig
sections.explanation: Just as you wouldn't configure Logback from inside a library, Python libraries should only obtain a logger with getLogger(__name__) and never configure handlers. The application sets level/format/handlers once at startup (e.g. logging.basicConfig(...) or dictConfig), keeping output control with the app that runs the code.
